name: Tamarack
summary: The deciduous conifer — soft pale-green needles in spring, fluorescent gold in October, bare in winter. Native to Pocono Plateau bog edges and cool wet hollows. Adds a structural element no other tree provides.
notes: Documented in PA Natural Heritage palustrine communities alongside red spruce, hemlock, and white pine. Seeds feed crossbills, siskins, and redpolls. Hosts ~75 Lepidoptera species. Long-lived (100+ years). Larch sawfly and casebearer cause occasional damage but rarely fatal. Use wire cage — needles aren't deer-favored but tender saplings are.

planting.aspect: Bog edges; cool wet hollows; north-facing wet slopes
planting.light: Full sun preferred; tolerates partial shade
planting.soil: Acidic; peat or mineral; tolerates poor drainage
planting.site_notes: Must have wet OR cool moist site — will not survive dry uplands. Source from Musser Forests, conservation district sales, or Cold Stream Farm. Pair with black spruce on actual bog edges. Wire cage protects against deer browse on young terminal leaders.
